query: Coenzyme A structure and relationship to pantothenic acid

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin B5 (Pantothenic Acid) -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin B5 is exclusively a precursor in coenzyme A (CoA) synthesis and thus only directly affects this metabolic pathway. CoA has a role in hundreds of human biochemical reactions, such as cell growth, intermediary metabolism, and neurotransmitter synthesis. [14] [15] The structure of CoA functions as a carbonyl-activating group and as an acyl group carrier to help facilitate these various reactions. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Nutrients -- Function -- Vitamin B5 (Pantothenic Acid)
retrieved_docs.content: Pantothenic acid functions as a supporter of the synthesis and maintenance of coenzyme A, a cofactor and acyl group carrier for other enzymes, and an acyl protein carrier in the fatty acid synthase complex. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin B5 (Pantothenic Acid) -- Indications -- Discovery and Isolation
retrieved_docs.content: The molecule's chemical structure was determined by Williams in 1940. Elvehjem et al and Jukes et al demonstrated that pantothenic acid was a growth and "anti-dermatitis" factor for chickens. In the 1950s, one of the functional forms of pantothenic acid, coenzyme A (CoA), was discovered as the cofactor essential for the acetylation of sulfonamides and choline. In the mid-1960s, pantothenic acid was identified as a component of an acyl carrier protein (ACP) in the fatty acid synthesis complex. Fritz Albert Lipmann won the Nobel Prize in Physiology and Medicine "for his discovery of coenzyme A and its importance for intermediary metabolism." [2] All genomes sequenced to date encode enzymes that use coenzyme A as a substrate, the biosynthesis of which requires cysteine, pantothenate (vitamin B5), and adenosine triphosphate (ATP). [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin B5 (Pantothenic Acid) -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Pantothenic acid is commonly found in the form of CoASH or peptide-bound 4′-phosphopantetheine.

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin B5 (Pantothenic Acid) -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Distribution: The absorption of free pantothenic acid occurs within intestinal cells through a saturable, sodium-dependent active transport system. When pantothenic acid intake is high, and the transport system reaches its maximum capacity, a portion of it may also be absorbed through passive diffusion. Consequently, when the intake of pantothenic acid increases by a factor of 10, the rate of absorption decreases to 10%. [17] Metabolism: The synthesis of CoA from pantothenate is regulated primarily by pantothenate kinase, an enzyme inhibited by the pathway end products CoA and acyl CoA. [18] Elimination: Pantothenic acid is excreted intact in urine, and the amount excreted varies proportionally with dietary intake. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Nutrients -- Fundamentals -- Vitamin B5 (Pantothenic Acid)
retrieved_docs.content: Pantothenic acid, or vitamin B5, is an essential water-soluble vitamin that acts as a key component of coenzyme A and phosphopantetheine, which are crucial to fatty acid metabolism. Pantothenic acid is widespread in foods. The AI (adequate intake) of pantothenic acid for adults is 5 mg/day. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Nutrients -- Molecular Level -- Vitamin B5 (Pantothenic Acid)
retrieved_docs.content: Pantothenic acid is a water-soluble vitamin synthesized from the condensation reaction of pantoic acid and β-alanine in only plants and bacteria. Pantothenic acid serves to transfer and carry acyl groups. [49]

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin B5 (Pantothenic Acid) -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: When proteins and peptides, such as those derived from ACP, are hydrolyzed, there is an almost complete release of pantothenic acid or pantetheine. Following the enzymatic release, the absorption of pantothenic acid or pantetheine takes place through a sodium-dependent multivitamin transporter, with a Km (Michaelis constant) value of 10 to 20 μM for pantothenic acid uptake in the intestines. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Nutrients -- Cellular Level -- Vitamin B5 (Pantothenic Acid)
retrieved_docs.content: Pantothenic acid is absorbed via active transport at low concentrations and passive transport at high concentrations in the small intestine. Pantothenate kinase catalyzes the synthesis of CoA from pantothenate. CoA plays an important role in the citric acid cycle in the forms of acetyl-CoA and succinyl-CoA. CoA can be hydrolyzed to pantothenate for excretion. Pantothenic acid is excreted in the urine. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin B5 (Pantothenic Acid) -- Administration -- Orphan Drug Use
retrieved_docs.content: PKAN is a hereditary condition characterized by mutations in the PANK2 gene on chromosome 20, resulting in an autosomal recessive inheritance pattern. The PANK2 gene produces the enzyme pantothenate kinase, essential for metabolizing vitamin B5 (pantothenate). These gene mutations disrupt the normal metabolism of vitamin B5, which is crucial for synthesizing coenzyme A within cells. Consequently, the impairment of this enzyme profoundly impacts energy and lipid metabolism, potentially causing the accumulation of detrimental substances in the brain, including iron. Currently, PANK2 stands as the sole identified gene associated with PKAN. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin B5 (Pantothenic Acid) -- Indications -- Discovery and Isolation
retrieved_docs.content: American biochemist Roger J. Williams isolated pantothenic acid as a growth factor in 1931. He derived its name from the Greek word "pantos," meaning "everywhere," as small quantities of pantothenic acid are found in nearly every food. Williams et al noted that a single acid substance could stimulate the growth of Saccharomyces cerevisiae and that this substance was a 'universal constituent of living matter.'

retrieved_docs.title: Pantothenate Kinase-Associated Neurodegeneration (PKAN) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: On the other hand, recent studies have found that the decreased global structural connectivity within the white matter, as well as the negative correlation of motor system-related tracts, primarily those between the basal ganglia, cortical areas, and the cerebellum, coincides with the concept of a general functional disturbance of the motor system in PKAN. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin B5 (Pantothenic Acid) -- Indications -- Off-Label Uses
retrieved_docs.content: Individuals with a pantothenate kinase 2 (PANK2) gene mutation are also likely to have a pantothenic acid inadequacy. PANK2 mutations can reduce the activity of pantothenate kinase 2, potentially decreasing the conversion of pantothenic acid to coenzyme A (CoA) and reducing CoA levels. PANK2 gene mutations also cause pantothenate kinase-associated neurodegeneration (PKAN). A common hallmark of individuals with PKAN is an iron accumulation in the brain, forming a pattern called the "eye of the tiger" sign. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin B5 (Pantothenic Acid) -- Indications -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Dyslipidemia is another field that can potentially benefit from vitamin B5 use. Since vitamin B5 is essential for the biosynthesis of coenzyme A, it plays a role in the metabolism of lipids. One study concluded that pantethine (a derivative of vitamin B5) lowered cardiovascular disease risk markers in low to moderate-risk participants. [10] These risk markers include LDL, HDL, and total cholesterol.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Water Soluble Vitamins  -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Pantothenic acid (B5) is a component of coenzyme A and fatty acid synthase, both of which are necessary for energy production and the formation of hormones. Deficiency is characterized by dermatitis, enteritis, alopecia, and adrenal insufficiency. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1260
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium pantothenate (pantothenát vápenatý) is a calcium salt of pantothenic acid that belongs to the vitamin B complex and is classified chemically among calcium compounds. Its primary pharmacological action is phosphate‑chelating activity, which contributes to metabolic processes that require vitamin B5. Calcium pantothenate is a common active ingredient in a wide range of oral multivitamin and iron preparations, including products such as “ASCORBIC ACID 25 mg / THIAMINE MONONITRATE 2 mg / RIBOFLAVIN 3 mg / NIACIN 1.8 mg / PYRIDOXINE HYDROCHLORIDE 25 mg / FOLIC ACID 1 mg / CYANOCOBALAMIN 12.5 µg / MAGNESIUM SULFATE 5 mg / ZINC SULFATE 10 mg / CUPRIC SULFATE 2 mg / DOCONEXENT 156 mg / ICOSAPENT 39 mg (Dothelle DHA)”, “BETA CAROTENE 600 µg / Vitamin A Palmitate 600 µg / Ascorbic Acid 60 mg / Cholecalciferol 10 µg / α‑Tocopherol 20.1 mg / THIAMINE MONONITRATE 1.7 mg / Riboflavin 2 mg / Niacinamide 20 mg / Pyridoxine Hydrochloride 2.5 mg / Folic Acid 1000 µg / Cyanocobalamin 8 µg /

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 2 -- Community 591
retrieved_docs.content: Niacin (vitamin B3) is a core member of the vitamin B complex and is routinely incorporated as an active ingredient in a wide range of oral multivitamin‑mineral supplements, from diabetic‑specific formulations to prenatal and general‑health products. Typical niacin doses span from low nutritional levels (~1.8 mg) to therapeutic amounts (up to 40 mg) used for lipid management in diabetic patients, and the vitamin may also appear as an inactive excipient in certain preparations. Within the B‑vitamin spectrum, niacin is biochemically linked to pantothenic acid (vitamin B5), which together support energy metabolism and co‑enzyme functions. Pantothenic acid’s calcium salt, calcium pantothenate (pantothenát vápenatý), is the biologically active form of vitamin B5 and belongs to the same vitamin B complex. Calcium pantothenate contributes to coenzyme A synthesis, cellular activity alteration, and phosphate‑chelating activity, and it is a standard component of many multivitamin and mineral

Pharmacologically, calcium pantothenate’s calcium‑based structure confers phosphate‑chelating activity, which can influence mineral metabolism. Clinically, calcium pantothenate is contraindicated in patients with a history of kidney stones, as calcium compounds may promote nephrolithiasis. Overall, the community’s knowledge emphasizes the pervasive inclusion of niacin and calcium pantothenate in multivitamin products, their synergistic roles within the B‑vitamin complex, their metabolic functions, potential formulation variations, and relevant safety considerations for patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 36
retrieved_docs.content: and specialized nutraceutical formulations. Vitamin A provides retinoid‑mediated vision and epithelial health; niacin delivers B‑vitamin‑mediated metabolic and neuro‑protective effects; calcium pantothenate drives co‑enzyme A‑dependent metabolism through its phosphate‑chelating activity. Their combined actions enhance antioxidant capacity, immune function and growth, contributing to comprehensive patient care. Clinicians should note that calcium pantothenate is contraindicated in individuals with a history of nephrolithiasis (kidney stones), and products containing this compound should be avoided in such patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 938
retrieved_docs.content: folic acid, cyanocobalamin, biotin), vitamin C, vitamin D (cholecalciferol), vitamin E (α‑tocopherol acetate), minerals (iron, calcium, magnesium, zinc, copper, manganese, selenium, chromium, molybdenum, iodine), omega‑3 fatty acids (DHA) and other nutrients (chromium picolinate, thioctic acid, ubidecarenone, choline). Several of the same formulations also contain calcium pantothenate (pantothenát vápenatý), underscoring the frequent co‑presence of niacin and pantothenic‑acid derivatives. Calcium pantothenate is the calcium salt of pantothenic acid and a member of the vitamin B complex classified within the B‑vitamin family and the structural class of calcium compounds. It serves as an active ingredient in the same multivitamin and mineral supplements that contain niacin, providing a bioavailable source of pantothenic acid that acts as a precursor for coenzyme A, an essential cofactor in fatty‑acid metabolism and energy production.
